title: Multi-Modal Biometric-Based Implicit Authentication of Wearable Device Users
venue: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security

method.input_representation: Statistical features (mean, median, energy, power, skewness, kurtosis, etc.) from 5-minute non-overlapping windows of coarse-grained biometric data
method.training_strategy: Binary and unary classification with 75% training and 25% testing split

evaluation.context: Achieved using KS-approach with hybrid biometrics (Calorie burn and MET) at a probability threshold of ≈0.95
Reported as a trade-off point where accuracy increases above 0.97
Reported for sedentary periods at low probability thresholds

evaluation_protocol: Leave-one-subject-out (binary) and unary classification tested on 25% of the dataset

reported_limitations: Performance degradation during highly active periods

usability.usability_issues: Longer windows incur long delays in accumulating data points

key_contribution: First use of three coarse-grained biometric types (behavioral, physiological, hybrid) for implicit wearable authentication
Demonstrated that hybrid biometrics (calorie burn, MET) are more effective than single behavioral or physiological sources
main_strength: Extensive in-the-wild data from a large cohort (400+ subjects) over a long period (17 months)
main_weakness: Lack of active presentation attack (spoofing) testing
User base limited to a narrow age range (college students)
insights.notes_for_review: The authors suggest that a retraining interval of a week or a few weeks would be sufficient to handle biometric drift.
